title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Public Works Committee met on May 11, 2026, approving minutes and reports, awarding a $126,900 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel, adopting a resolution supporting state transportation funding, and directing staff to develop $100/day fines for illegal sewer discharges. The committee also approved construction agreements for Military Road utilities and construction services for upcoming road projects.

agenda.item: Call to order
Pledge of Allegiance
Roll call
Public comment
Approval of April 13, 2026 meeting minutes
Acknowledge April 2026 water and sewer permits
CIP update
Street operations update
Utility operations update
Review 2025 Water Utility PSC annual report
Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding
Recycled base course production
Sanitary sewer discharge violation discussion
Water and sewer connection agreement for Radkkey Avenue duplex
Military Road construction agreement
Construction services proposals for Alderson/Jelnik and Bladel projects

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a local road improvement grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Schofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, covering over 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project cost. This 2029 project may be moved to 2028. Michael noted the village has received over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion also covered Weston Avenue repairs by Integrity Grading for road settlement issues, with the committee emphasizing continued monitoring.

Staff reported on spring activities including road limit removal, mailbox and sign repairs, curb line sweeping, and hot mix patching. Significant dewatering efforts were required along the Weston Avenue tributary due to heavy rain and runoff. Multiple culverts failed on Jones Street requiring additional projects. Equipment maintenance and changeover from winter to summer operations was detailed.

Josh reported significant clear water infiltration into the sanitary sewer system, with April showing 37 million gallons pumped into the distribution system but 53-54 million gallons treated at the wastewater plant - approximately 15 million gallons of clear water. Investigation found residents discharging sump pumps into sanitary sewers. The committee discussed the upcoming INI study by In-Pipe Technologies to identify problem areas.

Michael presented the annual report showing net operating income of $482,911, consistent with the prior year's $490,000. Water loss was 6%. The utility value increased to nearly $34 million with $6.7 million in infrastructure placed in service. The utility serves approximately 6,100 customers across Weston, Rothschild, and Schofield. Motion to acknowledge passed unanimously.

Michael explained this League of Wisconsin Municipalities resolution supports long-term transportation funding solutions at the state level. The village has decreased its public works budget in recent years and relies on grants for major projects while seeking recurring funding for maintenance and operations. Motion to recommend adoption passed unanimously.

Michael reported King Gravel submitted the low bid at $4.23/ton for crushing approximately 30,000 tons of recycled material, totaling $126,900. Analysis showed this saves approximately $11 per cubic yard compared to purchasing material, resulting in about $130,000 savings across 2026 projects. Motion to award to King Gravel passed unanimously.

The committee discussed enforcement of ordinances prohibiting clear water discharge into sanitary sewers. After debate about appropriate penalties, the committee directed staff to develop enforcement language with fines of $100 per day for violations, distinguishing between unknowing first-time offenders who should receive notice and opportunity to correct, versus willful repeat offenders. Motion passed unanimously.

Michael explained a duplex developer needs temporary water and sewer service via long laterals until Radkkey Avenue is reconstructed with main extensions. The committee added language requiring reconstruction no later than 2029, at which time the property owner would pay assessment for main extension. Motion to recommend approval passed unanimously.

The committee reviewed an intergovernmental agreement with Rothschild for Military Road construction from Wolfman Street to Helin Street. Weston's share for utility relocation is estimated at $94,860 ($6,156 sanitary sewer, $88,704 water main). Steam Construction is the contractor. Motion to recommend approval passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Construction Services for Alderson/Jelnik and Bladel Projects
discussions.body: Michael presented construction inspection proposals. For Alderson/Jelnik roundabout: MSA at $37,315 was recommended over Clark Dietz at $48,910. For Bladel Avenue: Clark Dietz at $73,730 was recommended over MSA at $70,400 due to having full-time experienced staff versus an intern. Motion to approve both recommendations passed unanimously.

actionItems: Award 2026 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel at $4.23/ton (estimated $126,900)
Adopt Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding
Draft enforcement language for sewer discharge violations with $100/day fine
Enter agreement for temporary water/sewer connection at 2230 Radkkey Avenue with 2029 reconstruction deadline
Approve Military Road construction agreement with Rothschild for $94,860 in utility costs
Award Alderson/Jelnik construction services to MSA ($37,315) and Bladel construction services to Clark Dietz ($73,730)
Schedule special assessment meeting for Bladel Avenue on May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM
Send water main flushing notices to residents
Investigate permanent pump solutions for Weston Avenue flooding issues
Obtain cost analysis of clear water infiltration for next meeting
Consider street lighting for Schofield Avenue from Birch Street to Camp Phillips Road in 2030 CIP

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its reorganization meeting, electing new officers and approving a $5,000 grant for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission also heard that a competing hot air balloon event in Marshfield has been canceled, potentially increasing attendance at the Weston event.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Roll call
Election of officers
Public comments
Minutes of previous meeting (April 21st)
Work product transmittals and educational presentations
Old business
New business - Grant request: Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Remarks from staff and commission members
Adjournment

discussions.body: Trustee Erling was nominated for chair and approved unanimously. Cushing was nominated for vice chair by motion, seconded by Jackson, and approved unanimously. Renee was nominated for secretary and approved unanimously.
The minutes from April 21st were approved unanimously. The motion was seconded by Renee.

discussions.item: Grant Request - Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill of 3308 Green Pastures Lane presented on behalf of the Taste and Glow Balloonfest, reporting approximately 11,000-12,000 attendees last year. He announced that the competing Marshfield hot air balloon event has been canceled due to permit issues, making theirs the sole hot air balloon event in the area. Commission members questioned room tracking, noting the application showed 20 fewer rooms than last year. The commission approved a $5,000 grant matching last year's amount, with a request for better hotel room data in the post-event report. Motion passed unanimously.

Renee reported on attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar, noting the candidates were impressed with Marathon County. The winner, Anastasia, sat at their table. This was the first time the event was hosted in Marathon County since the 1970s.

actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant to Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Taste and Glow organizers to provide hotel room usage data in post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved the site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and adopted a new salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection areas. Both items passed unanimously with the commission addressing technical requirements including gas pipeline considerations and environmental protection measures.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Open public comment period
Approval of April 13, 2026 meeting minutes
Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
April 2026 building permits
Planning and development department monthly project update
Commissioner remarks
Future agenda items
Adjournment

discussions.item: Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court Facility Site Plan
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department at 3600 Community Center Drive, which meets all B2 zoning requirements. Chief Jeremy explained that the building was rotated to face east due to a gas pipeline running under the parking lot, which significantly reduced fill costs. The facility is designed for 50-60 officer capacity (current staffing is 40 officers plus 4 clerical). Discussion included the alternate storage shed placement, with commissioners suggesting it be moved north to allow for future expansion. Commissioner Zagami made the motion to recommend approval subject to staff conditions 2-5, which passed unanimously.

Staff proposed a new inspection program with two annual inspections per facility in wellhead protection areas - one warm weather inspection focused on structural integrity and one winter operational inspection focused on material handling practices. Jennifer noted one existing facility near Sternberg that predates the wellhead protection ordinance and has never gone through the conditional use process. Commissioners agreed staff should reach out to bring them into compliance. Commissioner Lumper praised the program as well put together. The motion to approve passed unanimously, with an amendment to report back to both the Plan Commission and Public Works Committee.
The April 13, 2026 meeting minutes were approved unanimously. Staff approved certified survey maps, site plans, and building permits for April 2026 were also approved unanimously without discussion.

discussions.item: Planning Department Update and Future Items
discussions.body: Staff announced the first joint Plan Commission-Village Board steering committee meeting for the comprehensive plan is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. Commissioner inquired about residential TIF districts for workforce housing, and staff indicated Ehlers has prepared a presentation on qualifications that could be shared with the commission or incorporated into comprehensive plan discussions. Electric vehicle charging stations were noted as a future agenda item for fall.

publicComment: Jim Penson of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via remote connection. He asked the commission to use common sense on landscaping requirements for the police department project to avoid excessive taxpayer costs similar to the Green Heck Turner building. He also requested that the salt shed inspection program include annual reporting on site visits and results, noting that some zoning complaints are years old.
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive recommended for approval to Board of Trustees subject to staff conditions 2-5
Salt storage shed inspection program adopted for wellhead protection district with two annual inspections per facility
Staff to report inspection findings to Plan Commission and Public Works Committee next year
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility on Sternberg that lacks conditional use approval
Joint Plan Commission-Village Board steering committee meeting scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Staff to send email and calendar invite for comprehensive plan steering committee meeting
Electric vehicle charging station regulations to be addressed in fall

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an orientation meeting for new board members, featuring educational presentations from department heads on the Health Department, Social Services, Veteran Service Office, and Aging & Disability Resource Center. The committee approved April meeting minutes unanimously and received detailed overviews of each department's mission, programs, budgets, and challenges.

agenda.item: Call to order and remote participation confirmation
Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Discussion of operational functions required by statute
Review of standing committee ordinances and jurisdiction
Health Department presentation by Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka
Health Department budget overview
Social Services Department overview by Administrator Leonard
Veteran Service Office presentation by VSO Aaron Galindo
Aging & Disability Resource Center presentation by Executive Director Mike Ray
Next meeting date confirmation and adjournment

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavalli made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. The motion passed unanimously with all members voting in favor.

discussions.item: Review of standing committee jurisdiction and structure
discussions.body: Administrator Leonard explained the committee's mission, statutory responsibilities, and reporting relationships. The committee oversees three direct county departments (Veteran Services, Social Services, Health Department) plus affiliated entities North Central Health Care and the Aging & Disability Resource Center. Chair Boots noted upcoming strategic plan work will be a focus for this committee through the first two years of the term.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented an overview of the department's four divisions: environmental health, nursing (communicable disease and family health), community health improvement, and administration. The department has approximately 37.6 FTE staff and manages over 1,200 licenses. She highlighted new initiatives including public health vending machines stocked with free tick kits and naloxone, the Nurse Family Partnership program graduating its first two-year-olds, and a new FTE position for homeless system response created from a prior task force recommendation. Supervisor Kremes asked about pandemic preparedness, and Suginaka confirmed the department has emergency response plans and recently conducted measles outbreak training exercises.

Administrator Leonard presented on behalf of Director Christa who was unavailable. The department has nearly 140 FTE and a 2026 budget of over $22 million with approximately $7 million from tax levy. Leonard highlighted the successful Family Keys housing stability pilot program, noting it addressed root causes of housing instability including working with families who had never had a bank account or been on a formal lease. He indicated potential restructuring requests may come forward to address increased need in certain units.

VSO Aaron Galindo reported the office increased to 3.0 FTE in 2025 and had 8,149 interactions that year, up from 5,900. Veterans in Marathon County received $44 million annually in benefits and $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025. Galindo highlighted initiatives including housing stability work with Center for Veterans Issues, suicide prevention efforts (noting 7 of 30 county suicides in 2025 were veterans), and outreach to surviving spouses for DIC benefits worth up to $2,400/month. Supervisor Kremes asked about regional collaboration; Galindo explained state statute requires each county to have a VSO but veterans can work with any county's office.

Executive Director Mike Ray explained the ADRC serves four counties (Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, Wood) with about 60 FTE staff and 385-400 volunteers. He described services including information/assistance, caregiver support, dementia care specialists, falls prevention, and the Meals on Wheels nutrition program. Ray noted significant financial challenges as federal Older Americans Act funding has been flat since 2024 while nutrition costs increased 40-45% over four years, forcing meal and staffing reductions. Marathon County's 2026 contribution is $413,135. Vice Chair Rosenberg praised the culturally appropriate meal program at the Hmong American Center.
The agenda incorrectly listed May 29th as a Wednesday. Vice Chair Rosenberg caught the error, and the correct next meeting date was confirmed as Wednesday, June 3rd at 3:00 PM.

actionItems: April 1st meeting minutes approved unanimously
North Central Health Care to be scheduled for separate full presentation before the committee
ADRC Executive Director Mike Ray to present annual report with 2025 data to full county board
Committee members to email Chair Boots and Administrator Leonard with suggested future agenda items
Chair Boots to bring strategic plan discussion items to upcoming meetings
Next meeting scheduled for Wednesday, June 3rd at 3:00 PM

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees, passed several ordinances including mobile food vendor licensing and e-bike regulations, and discussed ongoing challenges with the farmers market relocation to Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026 and approved routine license renewals.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order, roll call
Public comments - Jim Pitel
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees (Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015)
Approval of previous meeting minutes
Acknowledge reports from boards, committees, and commissions with chair updates
Acknowledge reports from departments
Consent agenda - vouchers and license renewals
Ordinances - mobile food vendor, standing committees, nuisances, e-bikes
Discussion on remote meeting attendance policy amendments
Appointments to committees and commissions
Discussion on farmers market location at Kennedy Park

discussions.item: Public Hearing - Park and Recreation Impact Fees
discussions.body: Staff presented proposed increases to park and recreation impact fees from $300 per single family unit to $761, from $250 per duplex to $563, and from $250 per multifamily to $495, consistent with the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Jim Pitel commented that there is no real data justifying the specific numbers. Trustee Barb spoke in favor, noting the fees are justified as new families require more park equipment and space. Ordinance 26-010 was approved unanimously, as was Resolution 2026-015.

Chief Finkey explained this creates a reciprocal inspection agreement between Safer and municipalities including Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, and others. A food truck inspected by any participating department receives a sticker valid across all jurisdictions, eliminating redundant inspections. The ordinance passed unanimously.

The board debated amendments to section 1.08 of the elected officials handbook. Trustees Beck and others questioned whether the policy language matched what was discussed at the previous meeting. An amendment was made to strike sentences requiring health/safety/welfare justification for remote attendance, leaving a simpler policy requiring 12-hour notice, camera use, and prohibiting closed session participation. The amendment passed with two no votes (Herling and Lobsra), and the amended policy passed with the same two dissenting.

The president appointed Golden Mua and Stephanie Daniels to the CLIPS committee. Daniels was previously on Finance and HR. The appointments were approved unanimously. Openings remain on Parks, Finance/HR (two spots), and CLIPS (one spot). The board agreed to include committee applicant resumes in future board packets.

discussions.item: Secondhand Article and Jewelry Dealer License - Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC
discussions.body: Chief Hunt explained the license is required under state statute for businesses buying and selling precious metals, with merchandise tracked on a database accessible to police to prevent sale of stolen goods. The license was approved with an amendment requiring satisfactory background check completion and receipt of occupancy permit, as the building is still under construction. Passed unanimously.

The board discussed challenges with the farmers market's new Kennedy Park location after losing the Camp Phillips/Scoffield corner when the property owner decided to develop it. Concerns included parking on grass areas, safety issues, and traffic. Renee explained the market grew from 25 stalls at the original location to 53 at Kennedy Park, and vendors prefer having vehicles with them. Mont Miller Park was discussed but rejected as too remote. The board agreed the situation is evolving and may need to reconsider options if problems persist. Renee clarified the market operates at a profit and receives grants for the token program.

Michael Dolan reported warranty work on Weston Avenue, Fuller, and Scoffield Avenue from previous construction projects. He addressed the engineering technician vacancy, noting the specialized skill set is difficult to fill locally. Regarding Weston Avenue speed limit, a traffic study showed 85th percentile speed at 45 mph, but the matter needs to return to the board. The speed limit remains inconsistent with the ordinance over two months after the board directed a change.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 52 Street offered public comment criticizing the hiring of outside engineering consultants for $108,000 when an engineering technician position remains unfilled. He questioned why Lisa Beck was introduced as a Safer board member before being appointed by this board, calling it inappropriate government business conducted outside the boardroom. He also criticized the lack of a detailed Kennedy Park project report in the packet and called for greater administrator accountability. During the park impact fee hearing, Pitel commented that the fee increase lacks justification with real operational data.
actionItems: Park and recreation impact fees increased effective with Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015
Mobile food vendor reciprocal inspection ordinance (26-008) adopted
E-bike/e-moto ordinance (26-012) adopted
Remote meeting attendance policy amended to remove health/safety/welfare justification requirements
Golden Mua and Stephanie Daniels appointed to CLIPS committee
Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC approved for secondhand dealer license pending background check and occupancy permit
Compliance assurance plan approved
Resolution 2026-014 supporting long-term transportation funding solution adopted
Board of Review adjourned to June 15, 2026
Comprehensive plan steering committee meeting scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 6:00 pm
Electronics drive date corrected to June 6th
Staff to prepare intergovernmental service agreement with Town of Ringle for building inspection assistance
Committee applicant resumes to be included in future board packets
Weston Avenue speed limit study to return to board for action

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board of Supervisors held an organizational meeting where they approved committee appointments, elected Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to the Executive Committee at-large positions, and unanimously approved a resolution to reduce landfill tipping fees for storm debris following the recent tornado and flooding disaster. The board heard emotional public testimony from affected Ringle residents requesting fee waivers.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Reading of notice and roll call
Committee appointments by County Board Chair
Election of at-large Executive Committee members
Consent agenda (Items C10-C14A)
Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution 25-26: Social services budget amendment
Resolution 26-26: Modification to landfill tipping fees
Announcements and adjournment

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs presented committee appointments developed with Vice Chair Boots, noting goals of increasing diverse perspectives, developing new supervisors as leaders (34% are in first or second term), and honoring supervisor preferences. The appointments were approved in a motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Election of At-Large Executive Committee Members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated for two at-large Executive Committee positions: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg, who praised her advocacy for children and elders), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Vice Chair Boots). Paper ballots were distributed and Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the Executive Committee.

discussions.item: Resolution 26-26: Modification to Landfill Tipping Fees
discussions.body: Administrator Leonard and Solid Waste Director Hagen Busher presented a proposal from the Solid Waste Management Board to reduce tipping fees for disaster-related debris following the tornado that devastated Ringle and surrounding areas. The standard $65/ton rate would be reduced to approximately $36.94/ton after waivers from the state ($13.10), Town of Ringle ($2.33), and county reduction to commercial rate. The board suspended rules to consider this resolution directly. Two Ringle residents, Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck, provided public testimony requesting the county waive fees entirely, citing that 44 homes were affected and many insurance adjusters haven't arrived. The resolution passed unanimously after extensive discussion about costs, future policy development, and coordination with the Community Foundation's relief fund.
Items C10 through C14A were approved on consent agenda via motion by Supervisor Cavelli and second by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Resolution 24-26: Out of Home Placement Reserve Increase
discussions.body: Motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.
Motion by Supervisor P, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

publicComment: Two Ringle residents were granted special permission to speak on the landfill tipping fee resolution. Jessica Toasty urged the county to waive fees entirely, noting the state, DNR, and Ringle are waiving their portions, and that private businesses have donated over $40,000 in cleanup help while Marathon County has provided limited financial assistance. She requested a six-month fee waiver. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of homeowners with demolished houses, noting 44 homes were affected, some insurance adjusters haven't arrived, and residents need more time and relief from fees.
actionItems: Approved committee appointments for the term as presented by Chair Gibbs
Elected Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to Executive Committee at-large positions
Approved Resolution 26-26 reducing landfill tipping fees to approximately $36.94/ton for certified disaster debris through June 17, 2025
Administrator Leonard to coordinate with Community Foundation on potential reimbursement mechanism for unreimbursed insurance costs
Solid Waste Management Board to develop formal policy within six months for handling similar disaster situations in the future
Staff to track all disaster-related debris with certification forms for potential FEMA reimbursement documentation

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions and voted to forward a modified fee waiver resolution to the county board that will only waive reconnect fees (not other zoning or septic fees) for properties damaged by the April 17, 2026 tornado. The committee also received educational presentations from the Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning departments.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public comment
Approval of March 31, 2026 committee minutes
Public hearing: Tim Veland rezoning petition (Urban Residential/General Agriculture to Rural Residential) - Town of Green Valley
Public hearing: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture) - Town of McMillan
Consideration of fee waiver for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Educational presentation: Solid Waste Department overview
Educational presentation: Parks, Recreation and Forestry Department overview
Educational presentation: Conservation Planning and Zoning Department overview

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes with Supervisor Drebeck seconding. The motion carried with no opposition.

discussions.item: Tim Veland rezoning petition - Town of Green Valley
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from Urban Residential and General Agriculture to Rural Residential for a land division. The Town of Green Valley recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact and conclusions of law, seconded by Supervisor Kurt. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition - Town of McMillan
discussions.body: The petition sought to rezone from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy nature of the property; Garrett Pogle explained it currently has a home and horse riding stable, and the rezone would make the non-conforming home use conforming. No natural areas would be lost. Town of McMillan recommended approval on April 20, 2026. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve, seconded by Supervisor Drew. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waiver for April 17, 2026 severe weather properties
discussions.body: Director Lori Mskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and septic fees for tornado-damaged properties. Supervisor Drew questioned fairness to property owners not in declared disaster areas. Supervisor Drebeck expressed concern about precedent and costs. Eric Vogel asked if insurance would cover fees. After discussion, Supervisor Drebeck moved to amend the resolution to only waive reconnect fees ($160) for existing septic systems, not other permits. The amendment passed. The amended resolution was then approved to forward to the county board, though the vote was not unanimous.

actionItems: Forward amended fee waiver resolution to county board - waiving only septic reconnect fees ($160) for tornado-damaged properties, not zoning or other septic fees
Forward Tim Veland/James Offer/David Tract rezoning approval to county board
Forward Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning approval to county board
Town of Green Valley and Town of McMillan should update comprehensive plans to reflect zoning changes if applicable

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks Committee deferred discussion on allowing dogs at the aquatic center due to the chair's absence, approved installation of new kayak launch signs along the Plover River, and received updates on Kennedy Park development and trail maintenance. Public commenters expressed concerns about the dog day event and questioned transparency of Kennedy Park fundraising finances.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Roll call
Approval of minutes from April 27, 2026
Public comments
Kennedy Park update
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog Day at the aquatic center discussion
Graffiti on the Oaklair River Trail discussion
Yellow Banks kayak launch signs
Topics for future meetings and staff remarks
Adjournment

discussions.body: Minutes from the April 27, 2026 meeting were approved. A motion was made and seconded, with no discussion. The motion carried unanimously by voice vote.

Staff presented a timeline of the Kennedy Park master plan development as requested by the board. Director Sean Osterbring explained the village is responsible for approximately $7 million of the $14.3 million project, primarily infrastructure items like bathrooms and shelter. Scott asked clarifying questions about funding timelines, learning there is no fixed deadline but a 10-year evaluation period. The Friends of Kennedy Park have approximately $84-85,000 at the community foundation toward a $117,000 goal for field lights, and plan to make a public announcement at their June tournament. Luis emphasized the Friends group deserves grace as volunteers and encouraged community members to get involved.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Staff provided photos and maps showing tree removal locations along the river and installation of wooden construction mats over gas pipelines to protect them during equipment access. Dan explained the mats were provided at no cost and the village has extras for potential trail projects. The work addressed fallen trees blocking river passage.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the aquatic center
discussions.body: The committee deferred this item to the June meeting because Katrina, who requested the agenda item, was absent due to an unforeseen issue. A member of the public named Andy (6504 [address inaudible]) spoke against allowing dogs in the pool, stating 'There should not be animals allowed into a public pool. Period.' Andy indicated she may return next month to speak again.

discussions.item: Graffiti on the Oaklair River Trail
discussions.body: Luis raised concerns about graffiti on trail benches and amenities. Osterbring explained challenges removing graffiti from concrete surfaces, noting the pressure washer cannot access the trail due to bridge width limitations and soft ground conditions. Staff discussed potential solutions including anti-vandalism coatings and luster seal. Committee members suggested volunteer work days, noting community members likely would help. No formal action was taken; staff will investigate cleaning options.

Dan from Marathon County presented version 7 of informational signs for kayak launches along the Plover River. Three signs will be installed in Village of Weston locations (Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks). Signs include maps, QR codes for river gauge information, and location-specific history. The Highway J launch may be closed this season due to bridge replacement. The committee approved the signs unanimously by voice vote and will forward to the village board for approval.

Osterbring mentioned receiving a request to install food allergy awareness signs at playgrounds, noting one in 13 people have food allergies. The committee agreed to add this as a future agenda item for further discussion, acknowledging mixed feelings but wanting to be educated on the topic.

publicComment: Two public comments were offered. Andy (6504 [street inaudible]) spoke in person against the dog day at aquatic center proposal, stating dogs do not belong in public pools. Jim Pensan (5002 [street] Weston), speaking online under the name 'Vern M,' criticized the 4 p.m. meeting time as discouraging public participation and questioned the Kennedy Park funding transparency, calling the $8,700 equipment expenditure 'too coincidental' and asking where grant money and glow event proceeds are being tracked. He questioned whether the Friends of Kennedy Park are 'frauds' for not sharing detailed financials.
actionItems: Dog Day at aquatic center discussion deferred to June 22, 2026 meeting
Kayak launch signs approved for installation at Yellow Banks, Ross Avenue, and Highway J locations - forwarded to village board
Staff to investigate anti-vandalism coatings and cleaning solutions for Oaklair River Trail amenities
Food allergy awareness playground signage to be added to future agenda
Mock Miller Park master plan kickoff meeting with Rettler planned for following Tuesday
Bike and pedestrian plan open house scheduled for June 11 from 5-7 p.m.

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee held a routine meeting that included approval of budget reports and an educational presentation from the Municipal Clerk's office detailing election administration, licensing, and department operations. Committee member Evan Everson announced his resignation as he is moving away from Weston.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Roll call
Public comments
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
April budget status report
TIF 1 and TIF 2 detail reports for April
Legal details report for April
Educational presentation - Municipal Clerk's budget and office overview
Remarks from staff including farmers market update
Adjournment

discussions.body: Motion to approve minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting was made by Ernling and seconded by Daniels. Motion carried with no opposition.

Jessica provided an update on TIF bond payoff timeline, indicating they are anticipating October/November for final payoff. The CDA will need to pass a resolution to call the bonds. A committee member asked about final numbers and was assured they will be able to pay off the debt as planned.
Items 5, 6, and 7 (April budget status report, TIF reports, and legal details report) were acknowledged together. Motion by Daniels, seconded by Soaper. Motion carried.

Clerk Pam presented comprehensive overview of her department's operations including three staff members (herself and Sarah full-time, Leah part-time). She detailed election administration costs, noting postage increased from $6,000 to $15,000 for presidential election year. Discussion included Badger Book replacement strategy ($11,000 for four units), poll worker wages (currently $12/hour for inspectors, below average for chief inspectors at $13/hour). Pet licensing was brought in-house in 2025 after 12 years with Pet Data, collecting $17,480 through March with $8,067 retained by village. The quarterly newsletter costs approximately $30,000 annually for printing and postage to 5,089 postal patrons.

Committee discussed the increase in pet licensing fees from $10 to $20 for neutered animals. Pam explained Marathon County requested the increase because they pay about $100,000 annually to the Humane Society but were only collecting $65,000 from municipalities. Discussion also touched on the non-dog stray contract costing $175 per animal, including eight mice picked up last year.

Staff member Renee reported the farmers market at Kennedy Park has been going well with full vendor participation, new food trucks, and expanded offerings. Concerns were raised about parking on Alderson Street and Newport Avenue, with clarification that only the west side of Alderson now allows parking. Committee members noted congestion during concurrent baseball games and track meets.
Evan Everson announced he is stepping down from the committee as he and his wife are moving away from Weston, though staying in the metro area. Committee members thanked him for his service.

actionItems: Minutes from previous meeting approved
April budget status report, TIF reports, and legal details report acknowledged
CDA resolution needed this summer to call TIF bonds for October payoff
Chief inspector wages to potentially increase to $15/hour for next election term
Seek new committee members to fill vacancies including Everson's seat
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30 PM

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee met on May 4th and took action on several items including recommending approval of nuisance code amendments, an e-bike ordinance with emoto restrictions, mobile food vendor fire inspections, address corrections, and a raze order for a deteriorated structure. The committee also discussed but deferred action on overnight parking regulations.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Roll call
Public comments
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Written communications and department reports (Code Enforcement, Fire/EMS, Police)
Proposed amendments to Chapter 50 Nuisances
E-bicycle ordinance in Chapter 82 Traffic and Vehicles
Overnight parking regulations October 31st to May 1st
Crosswalk request on Alderson Street between Mount Olive Church and DC Everest
Mobile food vendors fire inspection amendment
Address corrections for E911 compliance
Raze order for 4104 Shore Avenue

discussions.body: Jim Senalt spoke critically about the new farmers market location at Kennedy Park, expressing concern that parking vehicles on grass violates village code and will destroy grassy areas. He questioned ADA compliance for handicap parking on grass rather than asphalt, questioned the need for fire inspections on food trucks, and noted that some code violations have been pending for years without visible progress.

Roman/Audrey reported 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from code enforcement as the property sale was completed and new owner submitted repair permits. Chief Hunt reported the police department had 2,394 calls in March (77 per day), 410 traffic stops, and 281 citations. The department has hired its 40th officer, with new hires starting in May and January. Two SROs work with zoning violations during summer. Motion to acknowledge reports 5, 6, and 7 passed unanimously.

The committee reviewed final amendments including tree limb height requirements (14 and 10 feet switched in text) over streets and walkways, with language added requiring compliance with jurisdiction authority. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Kimi to recommend approval to Board of Trustees passed unanimously.

Extensive discussion occurred about emotos (electric dirt bikes). Chief Hunt strongly recommended prohibiting emotos from roadways and sidewalks, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes. Committee discussed whether to exempt kids' play scooters but decided to leave officer discretion. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Sherwanka to recommend approval with amendment prohibiting emotos on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property passed unanimously.

Trustee Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour parking restriction (4am-4pm) is significantly longer than surrounding municipalities (most have 5-hour windows). Michael from Public Works noted plowing typically doesn't reach residential streets until 7-8am. Chief Hunt opposed eliminating overnight parking entirely, preferring uniform hours with other municipalities. Committee discussed various options but deferred action, asking Michael to identify specific problem areas to potentially sign individually rather than village-wide restrictions.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk between the church and DC Everest parking lot. Staff estimated basic crosswalk cost at $2,000, lighted system at $25,000-$30,000. Committee expressed reluctance to pay for crosswalks on request. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Sherwanka that if Mount Olive is willing to cover the cost, recommend board approval. Passed unanimously. Michael will contact Mount Olive.

Chief Hunt explained a regional fire inspection program with Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Rib Mountain, and Safer where any department's inspection is valid for all participating municipalities. One recent inspection failed due to improper LP piping (galvanized steel) and unsecured propane containers. Wausau opted not to participate. Motion by Sherwanka, seconded by Campkey to approve Ordinance 26-8 passed unanimously.

Staff presented three address corrections for E911 compliance: 5915 Delicoski Street (duplex needing separate addresses), Ross Avenue properties out of sequence, and 2211 Radkey Avenue (new duplex on private drive). Chief Hunt mentioned an additional issue at Weston Villa Apartments where each of 8 units has separate addresses. Motion passed unanimously to recommend approval.

Staff requested authorization to proceed with raze order for abandoned structure. Property has been abandoned since 2010-2011, was previously considered for fire department training burn but deemed too unsafe. Owners previously housed chickens inside the structure. Current owners prefer the village proceed with demolition; costs will be placed on tax roll. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke regarding several agenda items, criticizing the new farmers market location at Kennedy Park, questioning ADA compliance for handicap parking on grass, questioning the need for food truck fire inspections, and expressing concern about the age of pending code violations.
actionItems: Recommend Chapter 50 Nuisances amendments to Board of Trustees
Recommend e-bike ordinance with emoto prohibition to Board of Trustees
Michael to identify specific problem areas for potential overnight parking signage and bring back to July meeting
Michael to contact Mount Olive Church regarding willingness to pay for crosswalk installation
Approve Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspections
Recommend address corrections to Board of Trustees
Authorize staff to proceed with raze order for 4104 Shore Avenue
Staff to investigate property concern at Sternberg and South Timber reported by committee member

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

Approve the Minutes

Activities & Athletics Code (Action Requested)

Riverview RFP Approval (Action Requested)

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Call to Order
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Preliminary Discussion Regarding Potential Litigation 19.85 (g)
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate
Adjourn

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Call to Order
Roll Call
Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Reading of the Mission Statement
Excellence in Action: Maine Elementary
Public and Student Comment
Approve Consent Agenda (Action Requested)
Old/Recurring Business
New Business
Open Forum
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Adjourn

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.

The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

The commission discussed the final WPDES permit issued with a five-year term. This was a discussion item with no formal action taken.

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Arbor Day Proclamation
Week of the Young Child Proclamation
National Preservation Month Proclamation
Workers Memorial Day Proclamation
March 24, 2026 Regular Common Council Minutes
April 14, 2026 Regular Common Council Minutes
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.
